name: Baena and Porcuna Castles Loop
name_original: Albendín – vuelta por Porcuna y Baena
description: A difficult 49.2-mile (79.2 km) road cycling route around Porcuna and Baena, featuring historical landmarks and significant elevation gain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Baena traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills and extensive olive groves, offering a distinctive Andalusian backdrop. The region features a network of greenways, including the Olive Oil Greenway, which provides smooth, paved surfaces for cyclists. The Guadajoz River flows through the area, contributing to varied natural features, while the Vardomojón Reservoir adds to the scenic diversity. This combination of agricultural heartland and natural waterways creates a dynamic environment for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Baena The most popular road cycling route is Alcaudete — circular from Baena, a 37.2 miles (59.8 km) trail that takes 2 hours 59 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the rolling terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Climb to the Bat Cave - circular from Baena, a moderate 22.3 miles (35.9 km) path. This route includes notable climbs and offers views of the surrounding countryside. Local road cyclists also love the Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena, a 49.2 miles (79.2 km) trail leading through agricultural landscapes and small towns, often completed in about 3 hours 47 minutes. Road cycling around Baena is defined by extensive olive groves, rolling hills, and dedicated greenways. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Baena's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Baena?
A: There are over 60 road cycling routes around Baena, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. The komoot community has recorded 61 routes in total for the area.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Baena?
A: Road cycling routes in Baena primarily feature rolling hills and extensive olive groves, providing a characteristic Andalusian backdrop. You'll find a network of greenways, such as the Olive Oil Greenway, which offers smooth, paved surfaces. Some routes may also incorporate gravel sections, catering to diverse preferences.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Baena?
A: While many routes involve significant elevation, there is at least one easy road cycling route available. The region's greenways, like the Olive Oil Greenway, generally offer smoother and less challenging sections suitable for those new to road cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Baena?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, Baena offers numerous difficult routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996034274/albendin-tour-around-porcuna-and-baena" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena&lt;/a&gt; is a 49.2-mile (79.2 km) trail with over 1,000 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005712176/circular-route-from-porcuna-via-baena-albendin-and-higuera-de-calatrava" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular route from Porcuna via Baena, Albendin and Higuera de Calatrava&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 92 km with more than 1,200 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available from Baena?
A: Yes, Baena offers several excellent circular road cycling routes. A popular choice is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1016770570/alcaudete-circular-from-baena" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcaudete — circular from Baena&lt;/a&gt;, a 37.2-mile (59.8 km) trail with significant elevation gain. Another option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1008805818/climb-to-the-bat-cave-circular-from-baena" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to the Bat Cave - circular from Baena&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 22.3-mile (35.9 km) path.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes in Baena?
A: Cycling routes around Baena often pass through expansive olive groves and rolling hills. The Guadajoz River flows through the region, and you might encounter small endorheic lagoons like La Quinta. The Vardomojón Reservoir near Albendín also adds to the scenic diversity. For specific natural highlights, consider routes that pass near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5342358" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bat Cave&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1045186" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Limestone formations and caves in the Bailón Canyon&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by bike in Baena?
A: Yes, Baena's rich history can be explored by bike. You can cycle past the 10th-century castle overlooking the town or visit the archaeological site of Torreparedones, an open-air site with Iberian and Roman remains amidst olive groves. Routes may also lead you near historical castles such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5361618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zuheros Castle&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5394191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro del Río Castle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Baena?
A: The best time for road cycling in Baena is generally during the spring and autumn months when temperatures are milder and more comfortable for outdoor activities. Summers can be very hot, making early mornings or late afternoons the only viable options, while winters are generally mild but can have cooler, wetter days.

Q: Is Baena a good destination for family-friendly cycling?
A: Baena can be suitable for family-friendly cycling, especially on the dedicated greenways like the Olive Oil Greenway, which offer smooth, paved surfaces away from traffic. These routes provide a safer and more enjoyable experience for families. However, many of the region's road cycling routes involve significant elevation, so choosing routes carefully based on the family's fitness level is advisable.

Q: Can I bring my dog on road cycling routes in Baena?
A: While the region is generally outdoor-friendly, specific regulations for dogs on road cycling routes or greenways are not universally defined. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash and ensure they are well-behaved, especially when cycling near towns or on shared paths. For specific routes, check local signage or guidelines.

Q: Are there places to park my car when starting a road cycling route in Baena?
A: Baena, as a town, offers various parking options. When planning your route, look for public parking areas within the town or near the start of popular greenways. Many towns in Spain have designated parking, and it's usually possible to find suitable spots to leave your car while you cycle.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Baena?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise Baena for its unique blend of challenging climbs and scenic beauty. The extensive olive groves provide a stunning backdrop, and the well-maintained greenways offer enjoyable stretches. Riders often highlight the opportunity to immerse themselves in the agricultural heartland of Andalusia while experiencing diverse terrains.
tips: Stone castle and fortifications in the town of Baena, or Bayyana (Muslim name). Historical sources relate it to the muladí rebellion of Umar ibn Hafsun, in the second half of the 9th century.

The Plaza de Andalucía is the center of the town, where the Town Hall building and the tourist information center are located. The main building in the square is the church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción, built between the late 19th and early 20th centuries in neo-Romanesque style.

Boabdil Tower, an octagonal building built with quality masonry between 1411 and 1435, reaching 28 meters in height. Gothic arches and Cistercian-inspired vaults design the interior spaces or chambers, where it seems that King Boabdil was imprisoned in 1483, after being defeated and imprisoned in the Battle of Lucena at the hands of the Catholic Monarchs.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult road bike ride. It's a demanding tour that requires a good level of physical fitness due to continuous ascents and descents over its 79 km distance and 1062 meters of elevation gain.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: The Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena is primarily designed for road bikes, indicating mostly paved surfaces. However, given its challenging nature and significant elevation changes, be prepared for continuous ascents and descents throughout the ride.

faqs.question: What historical landmarks and points of interest can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers a blend of athletic challenge and cultural exploration. You can expect to see notable historical and architectural landmarks, including the Baena Castle, the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption, and the Torre de Boabdil.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, for a route of this length and type, it's advisable to look for public parking in the towns of Albendín, Porcuna, or Baena, depending on your preferred starting point. These towns are the main hubs along the tour.

faqs.question: Is the Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for road cycling. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces in Spain, bringing a dog on a demanding 79 km road bike tour is not practical or recommended for their safety and comfort. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular adventure.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in Andalusia, including this region, is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are milder and more pleasant for demanding rides, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential chill of winter.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle the Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena. It follows public roads and paths. However, if you plan to enter any of the historical sites like Baena Castle, there might be separate admission fees.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through the towns of Porcuna and Baena, you will find opportunities to stop at cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel and rest. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially between towns.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other paths. You will encounter small portions of the Camino Mozárabe, Camino Mozárabe desde Málaga, Vereda del Infante, Camino de Valenzuela, Camino del Pozuelo, and Cordel de Castro a Porcuna.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Albendin - tour around Porcuna and Baena?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this 79 km road cycling tour is approximately 3 hours and 47 minutes. However, this is a moving time estimate and does not account for stops at landmarks, breaks, or varying fitness levels. Plan for a longer overall trip to enjoy the scenery and towns.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires a good level of physical conditioning due to its significant distance and elevation gain. It is not recommended for beginner road cyclists.
